_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology and clinical course cross-verified against KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline on Glomerular Diseases (IgA nephropathy module), Lai et al. Nat Rev Dis Primers 2016 (PMID 26905828), Wyatt and Julian NEJM 2013 (PMID 23782179), Roberts et al. Kidney Int 2009 (Oxford classification PMID 19571790). Treatment guidance from KDIGO 2024 with NefIgArd (Lafayette/Rovin 2023 PMID 37837989), PROTECT (Heerspink 2023 PMID 37772707), and TESTING (Lv 2022 PMID 35759695).

h1Title: IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease): Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: IgA nephropathy is the most common primary glomerular disease worldwide, caused by deposition of galactose-deficient IgA1 immune complexes in the kidney mesangium. Incidence is roughly 2.5 per 100,000 per year in Western populations and 3-4 times higher in East Asia, where universal urinalysis screening detects asymptomatic cases earlier. The classic presentation is visible blood in the urine within 24-48 hours of an upper respiratory or gastrointestinal infection (synpharyngitic hematuria) in a young adult, with persistent microscopic hematuria and proteinuria between episodes. Without treatment, 25-30% of patients progress to kidney failure within 20 years, driven by persistent proteinuria above 1 g/day. Modern care combines maximal renin-angiotensin blockade, the newer SGLT2 inhibitors and endothelin/angiotensin receptor antagonists, and targeted gut-release budesonide for high-risk disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 2.5 per 100,000 per year in Western Europe and North America; 9-45 per 100,000 in East Asia
keyStats.demographics: Male:female ratio 2:1 in Caucasians; near 1:1 in East Asians; rare in people of African ancestry
keyStats.avgAge: Peak presentation age 16-35 years; can occur at any age including childhood and over 65
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 25-50 cases per 100,000 prevalence; the single most common primary glomerulonephritis on biopsy registries worldwide

definition: IgA nephropathy (ICD-10: N02.8), also known as Berger disease after Jean Berger who first described the entity in 1968, is a primary glomerular disease defined by mesangial deposition of polymeric, galactose-deficient IgA1 on kidney biopsy with dominant or co-dominant IgA staining on immunofluorescence. The disease is the renal-limited form of a wider IgA-mediated spectrum that includes IgA vasculitis (formerly Henoch-Schonlein purpura). Pathogenesis follows a four-hit model: hit 1, mucosal overproduction of galactose-deficient IgA1; hit 2, generation of anti-glycan IgG autoantibodies; hit 3, formation of circulating immune complexes; hit 4, complex deposition in the mesangium with complement activation and mesangial cell proliferation. The disease is graded histologically by the MEST-C Oxford classification (mesangial hypercellularity, endocapillary proliferation, segmental sclerosis, tubular atrophy/interstitial fibrosis, crescents). Clinically it ranges from isolated microscopic hematuria through nephrotic syndrome and rapidly progressive crescentic glomerulonephritis. Management is led by nephrology, often in combination with rheumatology when extrarenal IgA vasculitis features are present.

typesClassification.description: Microscopic hematuria with or without low-grade proteinuria, typically detected on routine urinalysis or screening in East Asia. Accounts for 30-50% of incident cases in screened populations.

Recurrent episodes of visible (cola-coloured) hematuria within 24-72 hours of upper respiratory or gastrointestinal infection. The most recognizable phenotype, classically in adolescents and young adults.
The dominant chronic phenotype after age 30; proteinuria above 1 g/day plus hypertension drives long-term kidney function loss and warrants treatment intensification.
Proteinuria above 3.5 g/day with hypoalbuminemia; may overlap with minimal change disease (so-called MCD/IgAN dual diagnosis) and sometimes responds to corticosteroids.

Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis with cellular crescents in more than 25-50% of glomeruli, rising creatinine over weeks, often requiring immunosuppression including pulse methylprednisolone and cyclophosphamide or rituximab.

primarySymptoms: Visible cola-coloured or red urine appearing 24-72 hours after a sore throat, tonsillitis, or gastrointestinal infection, lasting 1-7 days and recurring with successive infections.
Persistent microscopic hematuria between gross episodes, detected only on urinalysis with more than 5 red blood cells per high-power field.
Foamy urine reflecting proteinuria above 1 g/day, often the first sign noticed by patients.
Elevated blood pressure on routine screening, present in 40-60% of patients at diagnosis and worsening as kidney function declines.
Flank or loin pain during gross hematuria episodes, attributed to capsular distension or transient tubular obstruction by red blood cell casts.
Bilateral lower limb oedema and rising creatinine in nephrotic-range or rapidly progressive disease.
Fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, and pallor as glomerular filtration rate falls toward stage 3-4 chronic kidney disease.
Palpable purpura on the buttocks and lower limbs, arthralgia, and crampy abdominal pain when IgA vasculitis features coexist.
earlyWarningSigns: Microscopic hematuria detected on routine school, employment, or pregnancy urinalysis in an otherwise healthy young adult
Hematuria persisting for more than 72 hours after a sore throat or gastroenteritis settles in an adolescent or young adult
A urine protein-to-creatinine ratio above 200 mg/g in a patient with isolated microscopic hematuria
New hypertension under age 35 with bland urinalysis but red blood cells on phase-contrast microscopy
First-degree relative with biopsy-proven IgA nephropathy or unexplained kidney failure
emergencySigns: Sudden doubling of serum creatinine over days to weeks with active urinary sediment — possible crescentic transformation requiring urgent biopsy
Hypertensive emergency (systolic blood pressure above 180 mmHg with headache, visual change, or seizures)
Anuria or oliguria below 0.5 mL/kg/hour during a flare suggesting acute kidney injury
Severe headache, neck stiffness, or focal neurology in a patient on immunosuppression — exclude opportunistic infection
Bilateral purpura with severe abdominal pain, gastrointestinal bleeding, or testicular pain suggesting IgA vasculitis intussusception

causes.description: Plasma cells in mucosal lymphoid tissue (tonsils, gut Peyer patches) produce IgA1 with abnormal O-linked glycosylation in genetically susceptible individuals. Galactose-deficient IgA1 levels are heritable and elevated in 75% of patients and many first-degree relatives.
Patients generate IgG and IgA1 autoantibodies that recognize the abnormally glycosylated hinge region. These antibodies form circulating immune complexes that lodge in the kidney mesangium.

causes.cause: Mucosal infections triggering immune complex flares
causes.description: Streptococcal pharyngitis, viral upper respiratory infection, and gastrointestinal infection drive surges in mucosal IgA1 production. These surges underlie the synpharyngitic gross hematuria episodes characteristic of the disease.

Genome-wide association studies have identified more than 25 risk loci including HLA-DQ/DR, the complement factor H cluster, and CARD9. Risk alleles cluster in East Asian populations and are rare in people of African ancestry.

causes.cause: Secondary IgA nephropathy from chronic liver disease
causes.description: Cirrhosis from any cause reduces hepatic clearance of polymeric IgA and produces mesangial IgA deposition with or without clinical nephritis. Alcohol-associated cirrhosis is the most common setting.
IgA nephropathy occurs in coeliac disease, inflammatory bowel disease, ankylosing spondylitis, dermatitis herpetiformis, and HIV. These associations support a mucosa-driven model of disease.

riskFactors.description: Prevalence on biopsy registries is 3-4 times higher in East Asia and intermediate in Europe; the disease is rare in people of African ancestry, mirroring frequencies of risk alleles at HLA and complement factor H.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of IgA nephropathy or unexplained kidney failure
riskFactors.description: Familial clustering is described in 5-10% of cases. First-degree relatives have 16% prevalence of microscopic hematuria versus 4% in controls.
Peak incidence is in adolescents and young adults, although diagnosis is increasingly made in patients over 50 with persistent proteinuria.
Men account for roughly two-thirds of biopsied Caucasian cases and have worse renal survival, with hazard ratios for kidney failure of 1.4-1.7 in pooled cohorts.

Frequent tonsillitis, pharyngitis, or chronic sinusitis drives synpharyngitic flares. Tonsillectomy reduces hematuria episodes in selected patients, though survival benefit remains debated outside Japan.

riskFactors.factor: Coeliac disease and inflammatory bowel disease
riskFactors.description: Both conditions occur 2-4 times more often in IgA nephropathy cohorts. Gluten exposure may worsen mesangial IgA deposition in coeliac patients.
The single strongest modifiable predictor of progression. Each gram of daily proteinuria roughly doubles the risk of doubling serum creatinine or reaching end-stage kidney disease.
Untreated blood pressure above 140/90 mmHg accelerates fibrosis and amplifies the harm of proteinuria. Treat-to-target strategies aim for under 120/80 mmHg in most adults.

affectedDemographics: East Asia (Japan, China, Korea): IgA nephropathy accounts for 30-50% of native kidney biopsies because of universal school and employment urinalysis screening (Wyatt and Julian 2013).
Europe and North America: 10-25% of biopsies, with incidence roughly 2.5 per 100,000 per year (KDIGO 2024).
Africa: under 5% of biopsies, partly reflecting low frequency of HLA-DQ risk alleles and limited biopsy practice.
Children: less common than adults; pediatric series report mean age 9-10 years, with better long-term renal survival than adult-onset disease.
Pregnancy: IgA nephropathy may worsen during pregnancy in women with proteinuria above 1 g/day or eGFR under 60 mL/min/1.73 m2.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis starts with the urinalysis dipstick and microscopic phase-contrast examination of urine sediment — dysmorphic red blood cells and red cell casts confirm a glomerular origin and exclude lower urinary tract bleeding. Quantification of proteinuria with a spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio (or 24-hour collection in pregnancy) defines risk: levels above 1 g/g correlate with progressive disease. Serum creatinine, electrolytes, and estimated glomerular filtration rate establish baseline kidney function. Serology is largely used to exclude alternatives (anti-nuclear antibody, anti-DNA, ANCA, anti-GBM, complement C3 and C4, hepatitis B and C, HIV); complement levels are normal in classical IgA nephropathy. Serum galactose-deficient IgA1 (Gd-IgA1) and serum/urinary IgG anti-Gd-IgA1 are emerging biomarkers but are not routinely available outside research. Imaging with renal ultrasound confirms normal-sized non-obstructed kidneys before biopsy. The reference standard for diagnosis is percutaneous kidney biopsy with light microscopy (mesangial expansion, hypercellularity), immunofluorescence (dominant or co-dominant mesangial IgA), and electron microscopy (paramesangial electron-dense deposits). Findings are scored by the Oxford MEST-C classification, which feeds the validated International IgAN Prediction Tool used at KDIGO for risk stratification at biopsy.

diagnosticTests.test: Urinalysis with phase-contrast microscopy of urine sediment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms glomerular hematuria by visualizing dysmorphic red blood cells, acanthocytes, and red cell casts
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Provide a fresh midstream urine sample. The lab examines a centrifuged sediment under phase-contrast microscopy. Result available the same day.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio and 24-hour urine collection
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies proteinuria, the strongest modifiable prognostic factor
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A spot first-morning urine sample for the ratio; for 24-hour collection, urine is collected over a full day into a clinic-supplied container. Result within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes baseline kidney function and stages chronic kidney disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Result the same day in most labs; repeated at 3-6 month intervals.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms two non-obstructed kidneys before biopsy and excludes other structural disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard abdominal ultrasound, 20-30 minutes, no contrast or fasting required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive diagnosis with MEST-C Oxford scoring and exclusion of mimics
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case procedure under local anaesthetic with ultrasound guidance. Two to three small cores are taken using a 16-18 gauge needle. Patients lie flat for 4-6 hours afterwards. Mild hematuria is expected for 24-48 hours. Histology is typically reported within 7-10 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Complement C3, C4, ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA, anti-GBM, hepatitis B/C/HIV serology
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes lupus nephritis, ANCA-associated vasculitis, anti-GBM disease, post-infectious glomerulonephritis, and secondary causes
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood draw for the panel. Most results return within 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Calculates 5-year risk of a 50% drop in eGFR or end-stage kidney disease using clinical and histological data
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An online calculator used by the nephrologist; takes age, sex, ethnicity, blood pressure, eGFR, proteinuria, MEST-C scores, and treatment as inputs. Output guides intensity of therapy.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is guided by the KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline on Glomerular Diseases and centres on a stepwise approach to reduce proteinuria and protect the glomerulus. Step one is optimised supportive care: blood pressure target under 120/80 mmHg, sodium restriction to under 2 g/day, smoking cessation, weight management, and renin-angiotensin system blockade with an ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker titrated to maximally tolerated dose. SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin 10 mg or empagliflozin 10 mg once daily) are now added on top of RAS blockade based on DAPA-CKD and EMPA-KIDNEY subgroups; they reduce proteinuria 30-40% and slow eGFR decline. Sparsentan, a dual endothelin and angiotensin receptor antagonist, gained FDA approval after the PROTECT trial demonstrated a 50% reduction in proteinuria versus irbesartan at 9 months. If proteinuria remains above 0.75-1 g/day despite three months of maximised supportive care, the targeted-release oral budesonide (Tarpeyo/Kinpeygo, 16 mg daily for nine months tapered to 8 mg in the final month) is added; NefIgArd phase 3 showed a 7 mL/min/1.73 m2 eGFR benefit at two years over placebo. Systemic corticosteroids alone are no longer first-line because of toxicity demonstrated in the STOP-IgAN and TESTING trials, although a reduced-dose methylprednisolone regimen is an option in high-risk patients without serious infection risk. Rapidly progressive crescentic IgA nephropathy warrants pulsed methylprednisolone plus cyclophosphamide or rituximab in tertiary centres. Tonsillectomy is offered in Japan to patients with recurrent synpharyngitic hematuria. Kidney transplantation is the preferred renal replacement modality once eGFR falls below 15 mL/min/1.73 m2, with recurrent IgA nephropathy in 20-50% of grafts but graft loss from recurrence under 10%.

medicalTreatments.name: Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker (lisinopril 10-40 mg, losartan 50-100 mg, ramipril 5-10 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antiproteinuric therapy. Reduces intraglomerular pressure and proteinuria by 30-40% over 6-12 months. Combine with sodium restriction below 2 g/day for additive effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces 10-year risk of end-stage kidney disease by approximately 30% versus untreated controls in pooled observational data

medicalTreatments.name: SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin 10 mg daily, empagliflozin 10 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Added on top of RAS blockade once eGFR is above 20 mL/min/1.73 m2 and proteinuria persists. Provides additional 30-40% reduction in proteinuria and slows eGFR decline by 1-2 mL/min/1.73 m2 per year.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 44% relative reduction in renal composite endpoint in DAPA-CKD IgAN subgroup; consistent benefit in EMPA-KIDNEY

medicalTreatments.name: Sparsentan 200-400 mg daily (dual endothelin/angiotensin receptor antagonist)
medicalTreatments.description: FDA-approved 2023 for adults with primary IgA nephropathy at risk of progression. Started after stopping the ACEi/ARB; titrated from 200 to 400 mg. Liver tests and fluid status monitored.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PROTECT trial: 50% greater proteinuria reduction at 36 weeks versus irbesartan; 1.1 mL/min/1.73 m2 per year slower eGFR decline at 2 years

medicalTreatments.name: Targeted-release budesonide (Tarpeyo/Kinpeygo, 16 mg daily for 9 months, then 8 mg for 1 month)
medicalTreatments.description: Releases in the distal ileum to target Peyer-patch B cells producing galactose-deficient IgA1, with minimal systemic exposure. Indicated in adults with primary IgA nephropathy and proteinuria above 1 g/day despite optimised supportive care.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NefIgArd phase 3: 7.4 mL/min/1.73 m2 eGFR benefit and 40% lower 2-year ESKD/eGFR halving risk versus placebo

medicalTreatments.name: Reduced-dose methylprednisolone (0.4 mg/kg/day tapered over 6-9 months) plus prophylactic antibiotics
medicalTreatments.description: Considered in high-risk patients without diabetes, severe obesity, peptic ulcer, or active infection. Lower-dose regimen developed after TESTING trial original high-dose arm was halted for infection deaths.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: TESTING low-dose: 36% lower risk of 40% eGFR decline, ESKD, or death over 4 years; serious adverse events 11% versus 4% with placebo

medicalTreatments.name: Cyclophosphamide or rituximab for crescentic disease
medicalTreatments.description: Used in rapidly progressive IgA nephropathy with extensive crescents, in combination with pulsed methylprednisolone. Rituximab 1 g day 0 and day 14 is increasingly preferred for tolerability.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Renal recovery in 50-70% of crescentic cases when started within 4 weeks of presentation; outcomes worse with established fibrosis

surgicalOptions.name: Tonsillectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Offered in Japan and selected European centres to patients with recurrent synpharyngitic hematuria and chronic tonsillitis. Reduces episodes of gross hematuria and decreases proteinuria in some series.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces gross hematuria episodes by 60-80% in observational Japanese cohorts; long-term renal benefit remains contested outside Japan

surgicalOptions.name: Kidney transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Preferred renal replacement therapy when eGFR falls below 15 mL/min/1.73 m2. Living-donor pre-emptive transplantation gives the best outcomes. Recurrence of IgA in the graft is common but rarely causes early graft loss.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year graft survival 85-90% for living-donor and 75-80% for deceased-donor grafts in modern IgAN cohorts

alternativeTreatments.name: Omega-3 fatty acids (fish oil 3-4 g/day)
alternativeTreatments.description: Older trials (Donadio 1994) suggested benefit in slowing eGFR decline; subsequent meta-analyses are mixed. KDIGO 2024 gives a conditional weak recommendation against routine use.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tonsillectomy plus methylprednisolone pulse (Japanese 'Pozzi-Hotta' protocol)
alternativeTreatments.description: Combined surgical and immunological approach used widely in Japan. Reduces proteinuria and hematuria in observational cohorts but lacks high-quality randomized evidence for long-term renal protection.

whySeeSpecialist: Persistent hematuria with proteinuria or a rising creatinine warrants nephrology referral. The nephrologist confirms the diagnosis on biopsy, calculates the International IgAN Prediction Tool risk score, and decides between supportive therapy alone or escalation to budesonide, sparsentan, or systemic immunosuppression. Pregnant patients, transplant candidates, and children should be managed in specialist centres with experience of IgA nephropathy.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a nephrologist affiliated with a kidney biopsy programme and access to MEST-C scoring. For high-risk or crescentic disease, ask whether the centre participates in IgA nephropathy clinical trials and has experience with sparsentan, targeted-release budesonide, and rituximab. Renal pathology expertise is essential — biopsy interpretation drives prognosis and treatment selection.
hospitalCriteria: On-site percutaneous kidney biopsy programme with renal pathology
Availability of sparsentan and targeted-release budesonide
Multidisciplinary kidney clinic with dietitian and clinical pharmacist
Kidney transplantation and pre-dialysis education service
keyFacilities: Tertiary nephrology centres with biopsy and transplant programmes
Renal pathology services with electron microscopy
Kidney transplant and dialysis units for end-stage disease
preventionStrategies: Treat upper respiratory and gastrointestinal infections promptly to reduce mucosal IgA1 surges.
Maintain blood pressure under 120/80 mmHg with regular home monitoring.
Restrict sodium intake to under 2 g/day to improve responsiveness to renin-angiotensin blockade.
Stop smoking; smoking accelerates eGFR decline in glomerular diseases by 1-2 mL/min per year.
Avoid nephrotoxins including NSAIDs, IV iodinated contrast where possible, and high-dose proton pump inhibitors.
Vaccinate against influenza, pneumococcus, and SARS-CoV-2 to reduce infection-triggered flares.
lifestyleModifications: Adhere to the prescribed RAS blocker, SGLT2 inhibitor, sparsentan, or budesonide every day at the same time.
Measure home blood pressure twice weekly and share readings with the nephrology team.
Limit dietary sodium to under 2 g/day; read food labels and avoid processed foods.
Maintain a healthy body mass index of 18.5-24.9 kg/m2; obesity worsens proteinuria.
Limit alcohol intake to under 14 units per week and avoid heavy episodic drinking.
Use paracetamol rather than ibuprofen or naproxen for analgesia; long-term NSAIDs accelerate kidney decline.

recommended: Low-sodium DASH-style diet emphasising fresh vegetables, fruits, whole grains, and legumes
Plant-based protein sources (lentils, soy, beans) which produce less acid load than animal protein
Moderate protein intake of 0.8-1.0 g/kg/day in patients not on dialysis
Adequate hydration with 2-2.5 L/day of water unless fluid restriction is advised
avoid: Processed and packaged foods high in sodium
Sugary drinks and excess fruit juice; obesity worsens proteinuria
Large amounts of red and processed meat
Routine high-protein diets (above 1.3 g/kg/day) which increase glomerular filtration pressure

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise (150 minutes per week of brisk walking, swimming, or cycling) plus two resistance sessions improves blood pressure, glycaemic control, and proteinuria. Avoid extreme dehydration with prolonged endurance events. Patients with eGFR under 30 mL/min/1.73 m2 should taper intensity and discuss exercise plans with their nephrology team.
dailyManagement: Take RAS blocker, SGLT2 inhibitor, sparsentan, or targeted-release budesonide at the scheduled time each day.
Check home blood pressure twice weekly using a validated upper-arm device.
Test urine with home dipsticks monthly for new visible blood or heavy proteinuria.
Keep a record of any new infections, antibiotics, or NSAID use to share at nephrology visits.
Attend scheduled blood tests every 3-6 months for creatinine, potassium, urine protein, and HbA1c if diabetic.
Maintain influenza, pneumococcal, and COVID-19 vaccinations as advised by the renal team.
prognosis: Long-term outcome correlates with three risk markers at diagnosis: persistent proteinuria above 1 g/day, eGFR under 60 mL/min/1.73 m2, and uncontrolled hypertension. In contemporary cohorts treated with maximised supportive care, the 10-year renal survival is approximately 85% for patients with proteinuria under 0.5 g/day, 75% with 0.5-1 g/day, and below 50% with sustained proteinuria above 3 g/day. Histology adds independent prognostic information through the Oxford MEST-C score: tubular atrophy/interstitial fibrosis (T1, T2) and crescents (C2) signal the highest risk of progression. The International IgAN Prediction Tool at biopsy outperforms clinical assessment alone and is endorsed by KDIGO. Spontaneous clinical remission occurs in 5-15% of patients with minimal proteinuria. Modern combination therapy (RAS blockade plus SGLT2 inhibitor plus sparsentan or budesonide where indicated) is projected to delay end-stage kidney disease by 10-15 years in moderate-risk disease based on slowing eGFR decline by 2-4 mL/min/1.73 m2 per year. Post-transplant recurrence affects 20-50% of grafts but causes graft loss in under 10%.
recoveryTimeline: Proteinuria responses to RAS blockade and SGLT2 inhibitors appear within 4-12 weeks. Budesonide produces a 30-40% proteinuria reduction by 6-9 months, sustained 12-24 months after treatment ends. Crescentic disease treated within 4 weeks of presentation recovers function in 50-70% of patients; established fibrosis (T2 on biopsy) does not regress. Annual eGFR decline falls from 4-5 mL/min/1.73 m2 without treatment to under 1-2 mL/min/1.73 m2 with optimised modern therapy.
complications: Progressive chronic kidney disease leading to end-stage kidney disease in 25-30% of patients within 20 years; monitor eGFR every 3-6 months.
Resistant hypertension and accelerated cardiovascular disease — left ventricular hypertrophy, coronary disease, stroke; screen with ambulatory blood pressure and echocardiography.
Nephrotic syndrome with hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia, and venous thromboembolism when proteinuria exceeds 3.5 g/day.
Acute kidney injury during gross hematuria episodes, sometimes requiring temporary dialysis; usually recovers fully within 1-3 months.
Crescentic transformation with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis — rising creatinine, oliguria, and red cell casts; needs urgent biopsy and immunosuppression.
Treatment-related infections, hyperglycemia, weight gain, and avascular necrosis from corticosteroid use; mitigated by targeted-release budesonide.

supportResources.name: IgA Nephropathy Foundation of America
supportResources.description: US-based patient organisation with disease information, trial registry, and family support resources.

supportResources.name: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases Guideline 2024
supportResources.description: International nephrology guideline; the authoritative reference for IgA nephropathy management.

supportResources.name: UK Kidney Association RaDaR rare-disease registry
supportResources.description: UK national registry and patient information hub for IgA nephropathy and related glomerular diseases.

supportResources.name: International IgAN Prediction Tool
supportResources.description: Validated calculator used at biopsy to estimate 5-year risk of kidney failure or 50% eGFR decline.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus Nephritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus nephritis features positive antinuclear and anti-double-stranded DNA antibodies, low complement (C3 and C4), and a full-house immunofluorescence pattern on biopsy (IgA, IgG, IgM, C3, C1q). IgA nephropathy has dominant IgA only, normal complement, and negative lupus serology.

confusedWithConditions.name: ANCA-Associated Vasculitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ANCA vasculitis presents with positive MPO or PR3 antibodies, pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis on biopsy, and often pulmonary or sinus involvement. IgA nephropathy is immune-complex mediated with IgA-dominant deposits and lacks ANCA positivity and systemic vasculitis features.

confusedWithConditions.name: Membranous Nephropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Membranous nephropathy causes nephrotic syndrome with subepithelial IgG and C3 deposits and positive anti-PLA2R or anti-THSD7A antibodies. IgA nephropathy more often presents with hematuria, normal or low-grade proteinuria, and IgA-dominant mesangial deposits.

confusedWithConditions.name: Alport Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Alport syndrome is a hereditary type IV collagen disease producing persistent hematuria, sensorineural hearing loss, and lenticonus, with a family history of kidney failure. Biopsy shows characteristic basement membrane lamellation, not mesangial IgA deposition.

relatedConditions.name: Membranous Nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Another primary glomerular disease; commonly differentiated by biopsy and serology in the same nephrology workup.

relatedConditions.name: Lupus Nephritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Major immune-complex glomerulonephritis to exclude; both produce hematuria, proteinuria, and abnormal urinary sediment.

relatedConditions.name: Nephrotic Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Heavy-proteinuria phenotype of IgA nephropathy overlaps clinically with minimal change disease and membranous nephropathy.

relatedConditions.name: ANCA-Associated Vasculitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Differential diagnosis for rapidly progressive crescentic glomerulonephritis.

relatedConditions.name: End-Stage Kidney Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Long-term outcome of progressive IgA nephropathy in 25-30% of patients without modern combination therapy.

faqs.question: What is IgA nephropathy in simple terms?
faqs.answer: IgA nephropathy is a kidney disease in which an abnormal form of the antibody IgA1 deposits in the kidney filters. The deposits cause inflammation, leaking of blood and protein into the urine, and slow scarring of the kidneys. It is the most common primary glomerular disease worldwide.

faqs.question: Is IgA nephropathy the same as Berger disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Berger disease is the eponym for IgA nephropathy after the French nephrologist Jean Berger, who first described the entity in 1968. Both names refer to the same condition with IgA-dominant deposits in the kidney mesangium on biopsy.

faqs.question: What are the first symptoms of IgA nephropathy?
faqs.answer: The first symptom is often visible cola-coloured urine 24-72 hours after a sore throat or gastrointestinal infection in a young adult. Many patients have only microscopic blood and small amounts of protein in the urine detected on routine urinalysis without symptoms.

faqs.question: How is IgA nephropathy diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a kidney biopsy showing IgA-dominant immune deposits in the mesangium on immunofluorescence. Urinalysis, urine protein quantification, blood pressure, serum creatinine, and serology for lupus, vasculitis, and other glomerular diseases support the diagnosis before biopsy.

faqs.question: What causes IgA nephropathy?
faqs.answer: The disease is driven by overproduction of galactose-deficient IgA1 by mucosal plasma cells, followed by autoantibodies against this abnormal IgA1 and deposition of the resulting immune complexes in the kidney mesangium. Genetic susceptibility, infections, and gut-mucosal triggers contribute.

faqs.question: Is IgA nephropathy curable?
faqs.answer: There is no cure, but modern treatment can put the disease into prolonged remission and substantially slow kidney function loss. Spontaneous clinical remission occurs in 5-15% of patients. Combination therapy with RAS blockers, SGLT2 inhibitors, and budesonide or sparsentan delays kidney failure by years in many patients.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with IgA nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Most patients live a normal lifespan. Approximately 25-30% develop end-stage kidney disease within 20 years without intensive treatment. Modern combination therapy is expected to delay or prevent this in many patients. Cardiovascular disease becomes the main competing risk in advanced chronic kidney disease.

faqs.question: Is IgA nephropathy hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most cases are sporadic, but 5-10% show familial clustering. First-degree relatives of patients have a higher prevalence of microscopic hematuria (around 16% versus 4% in controls). Genome-wide association studies have identified more than 25 susceptibility loci including HLA-DQ and the complement factor H cluster.

faqs.question: Does IgA nephropathy go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Around 5-15% of patients enter sustained clinical remission with persistently normal urinalysis. Most patients have at least low-grade hematuria and proteinuria for years, and treatment is aimed at preventing kidney function loss rather than abolishing all urinary abnormalities.

faqs.question: What is the role of sparsentan in IgA nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Sparsentan is a dual endothelin and angiotensin receptor antagonist FDA-approved in 2023 for adults with primary IgA nephropathy at risk of progression. In the PROTECT trial it reduced proteinuria 50% more than irbesartan and slowed eGFR decline by 1.1 mL/min/1.73 m2 per year at 2 years.

faqs.question: What is Tarpeyo (targeted-release budesonide)?
faqs.answer: Tarpeyo (Kinpeygo in Europe) is an oral budesonide formulation that releases in the distal ileum to suppress Peyer-patch B cells producing galactose-deficient IgA1. The NefIgArd trial showed a 7 mL/min/1.73 m2 eGFR benefit at 2 years over placebo when added to optimised supportive care.

faqs.question: Can children get IgA nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pediatric IgA nephropathy peaks at ages 9-10 years and presents more often with gross hematuria after upper respiratory infection. Long-term renal survival is generally better than in adults, with under 10% reaching end-stage kidney disease in childhood.

faqs.question: Does IgA nephropathy affect pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Pregnancy outcomes are usually good when blood pressure, eGFR, and proteinuria are well controlled before conception. Women with proteinuria above 1 g/day or eGFR under 60 mL/min/1.73 m2 face higher risk of pre-eclampsia, preterm birth, and accelerated decline. Care should be co-led by nephrology and maternal-fetal medicine.

faqs.question: Should I have a tonsillectomy for IgA nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Tonsillectomy is offered in Japan and a few European centres for patients with recurrent synpharyngitic hematuria and chronic tonsillitis. It reduces hematuria episodes in observational studies but long-term renal protection outside Japan is uncertain. Discuss the trade-offs with your nephrologist.

faqs.question: Can diet cure IgA nephropathy?
faqs.answer: No diet cures IgA nephropathy. A low-sodium DASH-style diet with adequate plant protein supports blood pressure control and reduces proteinuria. Coeliac disease should be screened for and treated with a gluten-free diet, which may reduce mesangial IgA in coeliac-associated cases.

faqs.question: Does IgA nephropathy come back after kidney transplant?
faqs.answer: Mesangial IgA deposits recur in 20-50% of kidney grafts, but clinically significant recurrence with proteinuria affects 10-30% and graft loss from recurrence is under 10% at 10 years. Living-donor and pre-emptive transplants give the best long-term outcomes.

faqs.question: Is IgA nephropathy a form of lupus?
faqs.answer: No. IgA nephropathy and lupus nephritis are distinct diseases. Lupus has positive ANA and anti-DNA antibodies, low complement, and a full-house immunofluorescence pattern. IgA nephropathy has dominant IgA, normal complement, and lacks systemic lupus features.

faqs.question: How does SGLT2 inhibition help IgA nephropathy?
faqs.answer: SGLT2 inhibitors such as dapagliflozin reduce intraglomerular pressure, lower proteinuria by 30-40%, and slow eGFR decline. In the DAPA-CKD IgAN subgroup, dapagliflozin reduced the renal composite endpoint by 44% on top of RAS blockade and is now recommended by KDIGO 2024.

faqs.question: What blood pressure target is recommended?
faqs.answer: KDIGO 2024 recommends a systolic blood pressure target under 120 mmHg in most adults with IgA nephropathy, measured with standardised office or ambulatory technique. Home blood pressure under 120/80 mmHg supports the same goal and improves proteinuria control.

faqs.question: Do steroids help in IgA nephropathy?
faqs.answer: Systemic high-dose corticosteroids are no longer first-line because of infection and metabolic toxicity demonstrated in STOP-IgAN and TESTING trials. Reduced-dose methylprednisolone with antibiotic prophylaxis may benefit selected high-risk patients. Targeted-release oral budesonide is preferred when steroid action is needed.

faqs.question: When should I see a nephrologist?
faqs.answer: Seek nephrology referral for persistent microscopic hematuria with any proteinuria, visible hematuria episodes after infection, new hypertension before age 35 with bland urinalysis, or a serum creatinine above the local reference range. Early referral improves long-term outcomes.
